notes.anchor: with regard to the Kalends of Quintilis
notes.note: The first of Quintilis (July) was a standard quarter-day on which debts and dowry instalments fell due. Cicero must decide whether to risk paying out a large sum (Tullia's dowry to Dolabella) or to "break off" the marriage at that deadline.

notes.anchor: that breaking off you write of
notes.note: Abruptio: the dissolution of Tullia's marriage to Dolabella, with the financial consequence of recovering or forfeiting the dowry. Cicero leaves the decision to Atticus and to Tullia herself.
L. Egnatius Rufus, a banker through whom Cicero held funds; Cicero prefers to leave the deposit untouched rather than risk moving it in the unsettled situation.

notes.anchor: the man I am with is also in straits
notes.note: Pompey, in whose camp Cicero then was; Cicero had lent him a large sum, expecting it to redound to his credit once the war was won. Pompey's defeat at Pharsalus (August 48) made the loan a dead loss.

notes.anchor: On the Ides of June, from the camp
notes.note: The letter is dated from Pompey's military camp in Greece (Dyrrachium or Thessaly), shortly before the decisive campaign. Cicero, ill and disillusioned, would soon withdraw and return to Italy.
